variation_instruction.prompt: IMPORTANT: Think through the entire plan 2 times. Ask what could go wrong, and plan again. Only then should you start implementing. And then, when you do, implement from the inside out. Use the wishful programming approach. Write the internal code you wish you had, before you write the code that uses it.

llm_gate_results.reasoning: The implementation faithfully covers all requirements: (1) Consistent navigation with renderNav() used in all screens and a '← Terug' back button present in the game screen header; (2) No initial start screen — the store initializes with screen: 'mode-select' and main.js renders renderModeSelect() directly on load; (3) Score tracking via sessionHistory array with rollingPercentage() calculating over last 20 answers; (4) Game completion at 90% — isComplete checks 18/20 (90%) correct in last 20 problems; (5) Congratulations screen with 'Gefeliciteerd!' and 'Ander oefening kiezen' button routing to goToModeSelect(); (6) Greenish (btn--completed) and orange (btn--progress) borders with percentage badges on all selection buttons via scoreCardBtn() and getOptionStatus(); (7) localStorage persistence via loadScores/saveScores; (8) Dutch UI throughout; (9) Plain JS central store with createStore()/createGameStore() — no libraries; (10) Reusable components (AnalogClock, DigitalClock, SentenceClock, createElement, etc.); (11) Tests in test/store.test.js using vitest; (12) No comments or documentation in code.

The tests are comprehensive and meaningful. They include real assertions testing specific behaviors: store subscription/unsubscribe mechanics, gameKey string format, getOptionStatus filtering by mode/level/hourMode, game flow state transitions, level-specific time constraints, correct/incorrect answer detection, session history recording, score percentage calculation, game completion after 20 rounds with 90%+ accuracy, and editTarget/refTarget pairing logic. No tests are trivially passing without assertions, no tests were removed or weakened, and the assertions match the actual implementation logic in store.js.

Code review shows a well-structured UI with consistent styling, proper responsive design, adequate color contrast, and no obvious visual defects. The `.btn--primary` class is missing from CSS but base `.btn` styles apply correctly. Minor sublabel ordering in scored difficulty cards is cosmetic and not a layout defect.
